column1: 5;How do phone calls work?;"Short answer: a phone call turns your voice into electrical signals or data
column2: sends that information through a network to the other phone
column3: and turns it back into sound — with signaling systems setting up the connection and managing who talks to whom.

column1: - Capture: your phone’s microphone converts sound waves (your voice) into an electrical (analog) signal or into digital samples if the device digitizes immediately.

column3: quantized and compressed by a codec (e.g.

column1: - Signaling/Call setup: your phone tells the network you want to call a specific number (signaling). The network locates the destination and arranges a path so media can flow between the two endpoints.

- Transport: the encoded voice is carried across the network. In classic telephone systems this was a dedicated circuit; in modern systems voice is mostly packetized and sent as data packets (VoIP) or as data over cellular networks.
- Decoding and playback: the receiving device converts the electrical or digital signal back into sound through its speaker.
- Control and teardown: when the call ends
column2: signaling tells the network to release resources and stop charging.

column1: - Traditional landline (PSTN/circuit switching): calls were set up by switches that reserved a continuous circuit end‑to‑end for the duration of the call (guaranteed bandwidth
column2: fixed path). Digital trunks and time-division multiplexing are used in modern PSTN.

column1: - Mobile (cellular): your phone talks to a nearby cell tower (base station). The network routes the call through mobile switching centers and possibly onto the PSTN or the internet. As you move
column2: the network hands your call to other towers (“handoff”). Cellular systems have evolved from circuit-based (early systems

column1: - VoIP (Voice over IP): voice is converted into IP packets and sent over the internet or private IP networks using protocols like SIP (for signaling) and RTP (for media). Many modern phone apps and many carriers use VoIP internally.
- Codec: compresses and decompresses voice to use network bandwidth efficiently.
- Signaling protocols: SS7 (traditional PSTN) and SIP/IMS (VoIP and many mobile networks) handle call setup
- Trunking/multiplexing: multiple calls share physical transmission lines using time or frequency division or packet multiplexing.
- Switching/routing: systems that connect one caller to another — in VoIP its IP routing
column2: in cellular it involves mobile switching centers and subscriber databases (HLR/HSS).

column3: packet loss and echo affect call quality. Networks and devices apply jitter buffers
column4: echo cancellation and QoS to improve calls.

column1: - Security: modern systems can encrypt signaling and media (TLS

- Mobile-to-mobile call: your phone → cell tower → mobile switching center → routing through carrier networks (maybe across the internet) → terminating carrier/MCC → final cell tower → recipient’s phone. Handoffs and subscriber lookups happen as needed.

- VoIP call (app to app): your app registers with a SIP server → you place a call → SIP signals the other side and negotiates codecs → voice flows as RTP packets over the internet between the two apps (often relayed through TURN/STUN if NAT traversal is needed).

column1: What optimizer_switch is and how it’s represented
- optimizer_switch is a comma-separated list of optimizer “flags” like flag_name=on or =off (example: index_condition_pushdown=on
- Flags and their names vary by MySQL/MariaDB version. Default settings are chosen to be broadly safe; changing them can change query plans and may improve or worsen performance.
How to inspect the current setting

column1: How to change it in RDS

2. Edit parameters → find optimizer_switch → paste the desired comma-separated flags → Save changes.
3. If Apply Method is “pending-reboot” you’ll need to reboot the instance. If it’s “immediate” the change will take effect without reboot (depends on engine/version).

- prefer_range_scan — prefers range scans when possible.
- mrr / mrr_cost_based — multi-range read optimizations for range scans.
- index_merge — allows combining multiple indexes (can be beneficial or harmful).
- batched_key_access — can improve some index scan patterns.
- materialization — influences derived table materialization (MySQL 8 changed behavior).

column1: 83;There is a sound synthesis technique called Frequency Modulation wherein an oscillator (such as a sine wave)
column2: has it's frequency modulated by another oscillator to produce a more complex sound. Eg
column3: when the second oscillator is high
column4: the frequency of the first oscillator becomes faster. However
column5: in practice hardly any real musical synthesizers perform real FM synthesis. Instead they use a technique popularized by the Yamaha DX7
column6: and do what's called Phase Modulation instead. Please explain how Phase Modulation works
column7: and why it's used over real Frequency Modulation in most FM synthesizers.;"Short answer

column1: - Frequency modulation (FM): you make the oscillator’s instantaneous frequency vary with the modulating signal. In math: the carrier’s phase is the time‑integral of that varying frequency.
- Phase modulation (PM): you add the modulating signal directly to the oscillator’s phase.

column2: PM and FM produce the same spectra up to a scale factor and a 90° phase shift
column3: so they sound the same when you choose the right depth. PM is far easier and more stable to implement digitally
column4: gives nicer musical behavior (timbre is less sensitive to pitch)
column5: and avoids unwanted pitch shifts from DC or slow components. That’s why Yamaha (and most “FM” synths) actually implement PM behind the scenes.

column1: 84;Who would win in a battle between a hippo and a southern elephant seal?;"Short answer: it depends on the place and the individuals — there’s no guaranteed winner.

- Size and power: adult male southern elephant seals (the bulls) are enormous — commonly 2–3+ tonnes and up to about 6 m long — usually heavier than an average hippo. Adult male hippos are also massive (often 1.5–2 tonnes
- Weapons and fighting style: hippos have an exceptionally powerful bite and giant canines designed to deliver crushing
column2: bone‑breaking blows; they’re fiercely territorial and very aggressive. Elephant seal bulls fight with their teeth and sheer mass: they bite
column4: and use body blows and weight to dominate rivals.

column1: - Habitat and mobility: elephant seals are true marine mammals built for maneuvering in deep water; on land they are slow and cumbersome. Hippos are semi‑aquatic but excel in shallow freshwater and can move quickly over short distances both in water and on land.
- In deep ocean water: advantage elephant seal. It’s at home
column2: larger males can maneuver and use size to swamp or crush a hippo
column3: and a hippo would be out of its element.

column1: - In shallow water or on a riverbank/shore: advantage hippo. Hippos are more agile in shallow water
column2: have a far stronger bite and can inflict fatal wounds quickly.

column1: - On dry land away from the sea: advantage hippo. Elephant seals are awkward; a hippo’s bite and aggression would be decisive in most cases.
- Between average individuals of opposite sexes or smaller animals: outcomes are even less predictable; a big bull seal could beat a small hippo and vice versa.

Bottom line: habitat and the sexes/sizes matter most. A large male southern elephant seal would likely win in the open water; a hippo has the upper hand in shallow water or on land.";gpt-5-mini-2025-08-07;29.261495351791382;32.0;1492.0;1524.0;1088.0;True;

column1: 97;Explain why the 9th Amendment to the US Constitution is important.;"The 9th Amendment protects a fundamental idea about liberty in the U.S. Constitution. Here’s what makes it important:

- It says rights aren’t limited to those listed. The exact text is that the enumeration (listing) of certain rights in the Constitution shall not be construed to deny or disparage others retained by the people. In plain terms: just because a right isn’t written down somewhere doesn’t mean you don’t have it.

- It guards unenumerated rights. The Framers knew the government could not possibly list every right people have. The 9th Amendment preserves that reality and prevents the government from claiming that only the rights specifically named in the Constitution exist.

- It complements the 10th Amendment and federalism. The 10th Amendment reserves powers not delegated to the federal government for the states or the people. The 9th reinforces the idea that people retain rights beyond what is written in the Constitution
- It provides flexibility for evolving justice. Because society changes and new circumstances arise (privacy
column5: the 9th Amendment gives room for recognizing important rights that aren’t explicitly listed in older constitutional text.

column1: - It has influenced and supported key privacy and liberty discussions. In various landmark cases
column2: courts have invoked the idea behind the 9th to argue that there are broad
column3: unenumerated rights—such as personal autonomy and privacy—that deserve protection
column4: even if they aren’t written out in a single amendment.

column2: the 9th Amendment is important because it prevents the Constitution from becoming a rigid list of rights. It acknowledges that people retain many fundamental rights beyond those that are expressly named
column3: helping to preserve individual liberty in changing times.";gpt-5-nano-2025-08-07;17.564834356307983;30.0;2407.0;2437.0;2048.0;True;
